are an invitation to imagine.<|19.92|> <|21.36|> And I think this one is a good one.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is a particularly good example of that. There are indeed scary warnings in this tale,<|7.38|> <|7.38|> but there's also a farcical quality that demands that we engage imaginatively with this text.<|16.14|> <|17.40|> A king throws a banquet for his son's wedding.<|20.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.30|> Nothing strange there.<|1.42|> <|2.28|> He invites all of his best A-list friends.<|6.00|> <|6.70|> Also not surprising.<|8.02|> <|8.86|> But they would not come.<|10.78|> <|12.20|> We start to think there's something strange about this.<|15.16|> <|15.64|> No one turns down a royal encounter.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> invitation. The king has killed the fatted calf, and all of his finest things have been prepared<|8.18|> <|8.18|> for the feast, and yet the guests cannot be bothered to come.<|14.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.26|> The king sends out his servants again to invite the guests, and not only do some of them make<|6.02|> <|6.02|> light of it, we should think it very strange that the term kill the messenger really applies in<|13.06|> <|13.06|> this story. And the king responds with rage. That's understandable. But he also burns<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the city. And I just want to remind you that all that food is still sitting on the table<|6.24|> <|6.24|> waiting for people to come feast. Deeming the surviving A-listers to be unworthy, the king<|15.92|> <|15.92|> sends out more servants to go, therefore, into the main streets.<|21.40|> <|21.40|> Amen.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The ones that were just burned? I don't know.<|1.76|> <|2.82|> And find everyone and invite them.<|6.64|> <|7.60|> Indeed they went, and they gathered all whom they found, both good and bad.<|14.36|> <|15.16|> And the wedding banquet was at last filled.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As Barbara Brown Taylor asserted, I would totally be going to Luke's church.<|8.82|> <|9.90|> This same parable appears in the Gospel of Luke, and it ends in this very spot, with the wedding hall filled with guests who have said yes to the generous invitation of the king.<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.14|> But apparently Matthew isn't much into happy endings because he tacks on another ending,<|7.76|> <|8.76|> one in which the king is so upset that a guest has bothered to show up. I'm just going to remind you, this is a guest who was<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> pulled in off the street, last minute and not an A-lister, he is so upset that a guest isn't wearing<|9.32|> <|9.32|> the appropriate clothing that he orders the offending guest to be thrown into the outer<|16.40|> <|16.40|> darkness where there will be gnashing of teeth and weeping.<|20.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'll take Luke any day.<|1.94|> <|4.38|> I had the privilege of spending time earlier this week with Jerome Berryman.<|10.68|> <|11.28|> He's the creator of the Godly Play program,<|14.64|> <|14.92|> which we use with our children here on Sunday mornings at St. Louis.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> John's. And we're not the only ones. Godly play is used in more than 40 countries across the world.<|8.14|> <|8.86|> And Jerome is a sometimes parishioner here at St. John's. Jerome's premise of teaching children<|16.50|> <|16.50|> our sacred stories is rich and beautiful, and it invites creative and imaginative thinking.<|25.92|>
